channel.title: Gold on Здесь будет название
channel.description: Recent content in Gold on Здесь будет название

item.title: The Wit of Porportuk
item.description:
El-Soo was the most beautiful woman in the Yukon, and Porportuk was the richest man. The collision of these two facts would create a story that the old-timers told for years afterward, shaking their heads at the strange ways of love and money.

Porportuk had made his fortune in the traditional way — trading with the white men, accumulating wealth while his people grew poorer. He was old and fat and ugly, but he had power, and power in the North meant everything.

item.title: A Daughter of the Snows
item.description: Frona Welse was a daughter of the snows, born and raised in the harsh beauty of the Yukon. While other women of her generation learned needlework and piano, she learned to drive a dog team and shoot a rifle. The wilderness was her classroom, and it had taught her well.

item.title: The One Thousand Dozen
item.description: David Rasmunsen had a plan — a brilliant plan, he thought, that would make him rich without the backbreaking labor of mining. He would transport one thousand dozen eggs to Dawson City, where fresh eggs sold for outrageous prices. The math was simple: buy low in San Francisco, sell high in the Klondike, and pocket the difference.

item.title: The Men of Forty Mile
item.description:
The men of Forty Mile were a rough lot, even by the standards of the goldfields. This small settlement on the Yukon River was one of the earliest mining camps in the territory, established before the great rush of ‘98 brought thousands of newcomers flooding into the region.

These old-timers — the sourdoughs, as they called themselves — had little patience for the cheechakos (newcomers) who arrived with fancy equipment and fancier ideas. They had learned the ways of the North through hard experience, and they viewed the greenhorns with a mixture of contempt and pity.

item.title: The Gold Fever
item.description: The gold fever swept through the nation like a plague in 1897. Ships returned from Alaska carrying men who had been poor and were now wealthy beyond imagination. Newspapers printed their pictures alongside stories of nuggets the size of eggs, of claims that yielded thousands of dollars a day, of fortunes made overnight.
